← the Atlas

arivu — terminal awareness as an ephemeral surface on kaval

Features· seedling ·accepted ·JUN 20, 2026

A clean-slate design for kolu terminal awareness (git · PR · agent · foreground). Repeat the kaval decoupling one level up — the awareness sensors become a generic library kolu runs in-process locally (writing terminalMetadata directly), and an ephemeral daemon (arivu) on top of kaval serves the same awareness over ssh for remote terminals, mirrored + folded into kolu. One sensor library, two homes — in-process locally, a served daemon remotely. It ships standalone first (arivu + arivu-tui, the kaval-tui move one level up).

Contents

  • The core model
  • One library, two homes — and why only remote folds
  • The standalone tool — arivu + arivu-tui
  • Shippable slices
  • P1 — shipped (extract · finish the rename · the standalone tool)
  • P2 — arivu-tui --host (shipped) feature
  • P3 — arivu-tui fleet, the ambient multi-host agent dashboard feature
    • Why Bun, contained to the viewer
    • The fleet board
  • Status

kaval decoupled the PTY out of kolu into a standalone, minimal, durable daemon with a clean contract — independently shippable, independently driven by kaval-tui. This note does the same move one level up, for the part kolu kept tangling: terminal awareness (git branch/dirty, PR status, agent detection, foreground). A new ephemeral daemon — arivu (Tamil அறிவு, “the faculty of knowing” — a sibling to kaval, watch/guard, and odu, run) — sits on top of kaval, runs the sensors, and exposes its result as one typed @kolu/surface collection. Locally, kolu runs that sensor library in-process and writes terminalMetadata directly — no surface, no boundary. Remotely, the arivu daemon serves the same awareness over ssh, which kolu mirrors and folds in. One sensor library, two homes — and kolu becomes a consumer of awareness, never the place it tangles.

The core model

One thesis: awareness is a derived surface, and the thing that produces it is a standalone daemon on top of kaval. Three claims follow, each verified against the shipped code.

  • The unit is a generic awareness value, not kolu’s record. arivu exposes Collection<TerminalId, AwarenessValue>, where AwarenessValue is exactly the fields the sensors compute — cwd · git · lastAgentCommand · lastActivityAt plus pr · agent · foreground. This schema is generic: it lives in @kolu/terminal-awareness, composed from the vendor-neutral anyagent / anyforge / kolu-git shapes, with no kolu-specific content at all. The one kolu concept — location (the endpoint discriminator) — is simply absent (no sensor authors it; a remote tool can’t know its own kolu-side hostId). Kolu’s own TerminalServerMetadata is then just AwarenessValue plus location and the client/UI fields (themeName, intent, …): the awareness value isn’t carved out of kolu’s record — kolu’s record is built on top of it.
  • kaval stays byte-for-byte minimal. arivu dials kaval as a ptyHostSurface client for the four taps + getScreenText; it adds zero awareness/git/gh logic to kaval. The decoupling is additive — a layer above, never a fork.
  • Ephemerality is the simplifier. Unlike a PTY, awareness never has to survive a restart — it’s re-derivable from live taps + current host fs. So arivu sheds all of kaval’s durability machinery: no single-instance lock, no spawn/cleanup (it owns no PTYs), no persisted list (it borrows kaval’s terminal.list), no adoption or reconnect-survival. Every (re)start just re-runs the sensors and recomputes from now.

The payoff: clients keep reading the one terminalMetadata collection they always did. The awareness surface is an internal seam, not a new thing the browser learns.

One library, two homes — and why only remote folds

Local and remote share the sensor library and one schema — but they are genuinely different paths, not “only the transport.” The surface, and the fold, exist only where awareness crosses a process boundary:

  • Local → the sensor library, in-process (no surface). kolu-server runs the sensors in-process and writes terminalMetadata directly through the existing mutators — no surface, no directLink, no fold. The sensors and the record live in the same process, so there is nothing to serve and nothing to merge; wrapping them in an in-process surface would be the fictional boundary rejected in #1406. Local stays this way for good — the in-process sensors are always the running build, so awareness is current by construction.

Even after remote ships (P2), local does NOT switch to dialing a local arivu “for consistency.” That would trade freshness-by-construction and a plain function call for a separate process, per-delta serialization, and a fold — pure ceremony to move data within one process. The consistency that matters is already there: local and remote share the same library, schema, and AwarenessValue contract; only the transport differs, and it differs because the process boundary is real remotely and absent locally. if local → in-process; else → mirror + fold is the honest shape, with the cheap path on the common case. (A pure-consumer TUI-kolu that runs no sensors itself is the one shape that would dial a local arivu — a different product, not kolu-server.)

  • Remote → serve (arivu’s side), then mirror + fold (kolu’s side). arivu is Nix-provisioned over ssh (the same staleKey recipe kaval already uses), runs the identical sensors, and serves the slice over stdioLink — that serve side is arivu’s. The kolu-side mirror + fold — mirroring with the already-shipped mirrorRemoteCollection, then folding each upsert into the terminal’s terminalMetadata entry across the persisted/live fence, with the host-stamped location — is not arivu’s concern; it is remote-terminals R-2’s, and the fold’s structural costs live in that note.

So the asymmetry is real, and confined to the wire. Local is a closure-mutate straight to terminalMetadata; remote is closure → frame → serve (and then, kolu-side, mirror → fold). They share the invariant sensor library and one schema — but they are two genuinely different paths, and pretending otherwise hides the one new artifact (the host-side hooks impl that reifies each mutate closure into a serialized slice frame — a wire carries values, not closures).

No @kolu/surface change is required. The remote serve + mirror path already ships end-to-end, proven by the remote-process-monitor example (a surface served over stdioLink, mirrored back via mirrorRemoteCollection). A future graduation — mirrorRemoteSurface, the spec-driven dual of implementSurface that drives every primitive of a served surface into a local ctx — is attractive electricity (the demo would delete its hand-rolled bridge loop), but it is not on the critical path and it does not dissolve the fold; that graduation is now tracked in remote-terminals.

The standalone tool — arivu + arivu-tui

Like kaval, the daemon is a deliverable in its own right, shipped and proven before kolu touches it. Where kaval-tui shows what’s running in each PTY, arivu-tui shows what each terminal is in — a host-local “what is every agent doing, across every repo” dashboard with zero kolu-server dependency, dialable over ssh against a prod box.

arivu-tui · ssh nix@prod
$ arivu-tui list --host prod
ID BRANCH DIRTY PR AGENT FOREGROUND
a3f1 feat/dial-ssh ● #1412 ✓ claude · working node
b7c2 master · — codex · waiting codex
c9d4 fix/fold ● #1408 ✗ — nvim
# derived intelligence over the raw PTY — and a future TUI-kolu consumes the same daemon

It ships self-contained: the awareness sensors (now @kolu/terminal-awareness/sensors.ts (extracted in P1a)) import nothing from kolu-server or kolu-common but a logger, and the host runtime deps are just node · git · gh (SQLite via Node’s built-in node:sqlite — no native addon), so the bin travels cleanly over ssh. Defining the awareness contract once yields its consumers off one schema: the daemon (serves it), the TUI (reads it), and — remotely — kolu’s mirror. Locally, kolu uses the sensor library directly and never touches the surface.

Shippable slices

Each slice ships and is provable on its own; the long pole is P1a → P1b → P1c → P2 → P3. P1a, P1b, and P1c are the three parts of one P1 (the standalone story; detailed below) — extract the library, finish the naming, then build the daemon on top. P1a and P1b are behavior-preserving refactors. P2 is the standalone arivu-tui --host remote viewer; P3 turns that viewer into an ambient, glance-from-across-the-room multi-host dashboard, shipping as three PRs: PR1 (swap the viewer’s runtime to Bun, behaviour-preserving), PR2a (the OpenTUI render swap — the live list against one endpoint at a time, the already-shipped local --socket or single --host, with a one-second clock as the liveness proof; drop list/watch), then PR2b (the multi-host fleet board: fan-out + mirror + the cross-fleet needs-you sort). The kolu mirror + fold is not an arivu phase — it is how remote-terminals R-2 consumes arivu, and it now lives in that note.

PhaseKindShipsRetires the risk of…
P1a #1413 refactorextract @kolu/terminal-awareness — the awareness sensors + the generic schemas, decoupled from kolu-common; kolu-server + kolu-common re-point at it, and the package’s own surface adopts the sensor · signal · sink vocabulary (startAwareness)the kolu coupling — a clean generic package, behavior-preserving (green CI is the proof; kolu keeps writing terminalMetadata in-process, byte-identical to today)
P1b #1419 refactorfinish the rename in the leaves — provider → adapter across anyagent · anyforge · the agent/forge integration packages (symbols, module + file names, exports): the per-vendor plugin becomes the adapter, finishing the spine P1a half-shipped (FORGE_ADAPTERS still keyed a PrProvider) — zero new vocabulary. The live Watcher handle and the wire provider discriminant staythe cross-package naming overload (“provider” meant several things); one coherent, behavior-preserving rename with its own review, off the awareness critical path
P1c #1428 featurearivu + arivu-tui standalone — the daemon dials kaval and serves the awareness surface; the TUI viewer reads it — zero kolu-server involvementdo the sensors run as a separate, kaval-dialing process and produce correct awareness with no in-process handle? + ships the independently-useful tool, before kolu depends on a line of it
P2 #1439 featurearivu-tui --host — the standalone remote viewer: dial + Nix-provision a remote arivu over ssh, render the same dashboard. A one-level-up clone of the shipped kaval-tui --host; zero kolu-server. The shared one-shot dial graduated to @kolu/surface-nix-host’s dialAgentOnce.the cross-arch Nix provisioning of the arivu daemon over ssh — the substrate the fleet dashboard reuses — before kolu depends on a line of it
P3 · PR1 #1470 featurere-platform arivu-tui onto Bun via bun2nix — adopt drishti’s Bun-on-Nix recipe now (bun2nix pinned via npins and resolved with builtins.getFlake — no flake input, the same path odu rides, so kolu’s zero-flake-input rule holds — a bun.lock + autogenerated bun.nix dep cache, @kolu/* hydrated from the store), and run today’s list / watch unchanged under Bun. Behaviour-preserving in the output; no OpenTUI, no Solid, no new runtime npm deps.standing up a real Bun-on-Nix foundation in isolation — so PR2a’s OpenTUI bundle lands on a proven bun2nix base, not a throwaway wrapper swap
P3 · PR2a #1479 featurethe OpenTUI render swap (one endpoint) — retire list / watch and the columnify text; render a simple OpenTUI list of one endpoint’s terminals — the already-shipped local --socket (P1c) or single --host (P2), reused verbatim, no new connection capability — with a one-second clock beside it as the liveness proof (the SolidJS-canonical createSignal + onMount/setInterval loop, repainting only the changed cell). The terminal list is a one-shot snapshot — no mirror-delta wiring yet. arivu-tui --json dumps the awareness JSON. Adds @opentui/* + solid-js and the build.ts bundle; zero kolu-server, viewer-only Bun, the daemon stays Node.the Bun → OpenTUI(FFI/Zig) → Solid render pipeline itself, on PR1’s bun2nix base — proven behind a trivial UI before any data dynamism or fan-out
P3 · PR2bfeaturethe multi-host fleet board — fan P2’s per-host dial over N hosts, mirrorRemoteCollection each awareness collection into one aggregate keyed by (host, terminalId), make the list go live (repaint on any mirror delta), float every awaiting_user agent to the top across the fleet, and render the per-host groups + the breathing alert strip + the honest unreachable/skew/empty states. Zero kolu-server, viewer-only Bun, the daemon stays Node. (Optional follow-on: an arivu-board web surface.)the fan-out + cross-host aggregation join — arivu’s surface as a second-order receptacle a different multi-host app plugs into

P1 — shipped (extract · finish the rename · the standalone tool)

P1 shipped in three parts. P1a extracted @kolu/terminal-awareness — the sensor library — and inverted the schema home (kolu keeps running the sensors in-process, behaviour-preserving). P1b finished the provider → adapter rename through the anyagent/anyforge leaves. P1c built the two standalone binaries arivu (daemon) + arivu-tui (viewer) on top, needing no kolu-server at runtime. The local story was complete at P1a. (See the shipped PRs in the table above.) The two diagrams below remain the enduring reference — the build-time package graph, then the P1 runtime:

P2 — arivu-tui --host (shipped) feature #1439

arivu-tui list --host <ssh> (and watch) dials a remote arivu daemon over ssh, Nix-provisions it cross-arch, and renders the same awareness dashboard the local --socket path does — zero kolu-server, the read-only viewer the only local process. A one-level-up clone of the shipped kaval-tui --host, riding @kolu/surface-nix-host’s reach + provision machinery; arivu --stdio (built in P1c) is the serve seam the ssh dial speaks to. The provisioned arivu discovers the host’s kaval (the same discoverPtyHostSockets scan kaval-tui uses — a standalone daemon, or a kolu-server namespaced by listen port), so --host lands on a real remote kolu’s terminals with no flag; --kaval <path> pins one when several run.

The review gauntlet pulled the shared one-shot reach → provision → pin → probe → markConnected → dispose composition — until then duplicated between kaval-tui and arivu-tui — up into @kolu/surface-nix-host as a new dialAgentOnce primitive. Each CLI is now a ~10-line wrapper supplying only its volatile axes: the binary, the drv-map env var, and a connectivity probe (system.heartbeat for kaval; a version-cell read for arivu, which has no system namespace). The receptacle that already encapsulated the hard ssh/reconnect/provision volatility now owns the one-shot socket every --host consumer needs, instead of each one re-wiring it on top. (Because this adds public API to the drishti-shared @kolu/surface* packages — dialAgentOnce + its types, the new @kolu/surface/first-frame export, and the HostSession state/options changes — .claude/rules/surface.md and packages/AGENTS.md require a paired drishti PR opened before or alongside merge and green before “done”, linked from this PR. Mechanically the mirror is small — an npins kolu pin-bump plus hydrating the new zero-dep @kolu/shell-quote leaf; drishti’s source imports none of the new symbols, so it is a zero-source-change, green-CI confirmation — but it is a merge gate, not a post-merge follow-up.)

Retired: that the cross-arch arivu-daemon Nix closure provisions and travels over ssh (the substrate P3’s fleet dial reuses), and the version-cell-as-markConnected seam — proven by mocked unit tests plus a live capture: arivu-tui list --host <pu box> provisioning arivu onto a leased pool box and rendering the box’s kaval terminals (branch · foreground, with the gh sensor degrading gracefully where unauthenticated).

P3 — arivu-tui fleet, the ambient multi-host agent dashboard feature

P2 made one remote host’s awareness reachable. P3’s end state is the user’s framing made literal — “what is every agent doing, across every repo, across every machine” — as a dashboard you leave open on a second monitor: glance over from the game on the main screen and the board tells you, at a colour you can read across the room, whether any agent is blocked on you and where. It stays a TUI, runs zero kolu-server, and ships as three PRs — the Bun runtime, then the OpenTUI render swap on one host, then the multi-host fleet:

  • PR1 — Bun runtime, via bun2nix. Re-platform arivu-tui from the shared tsx wrapper onto Bun, adopting drishti’s Bun-on-Nix recipe now rather than deferring it: bun2nix pinned via npins and resolved with builtins.getFlake keyed off that pin — not a flake input, the same way odu is consumed, so kolu’s zero-flake-input rule holds and the dev shell never forces it — an isolated bun.lock + autogenerated bun.nix dep cache kept outside the pnpm workspace, and the @kolu/* viewer closure hydrated from the Nix store (never bun-installed — terminal-awareness’s full manifest drags the sensor spine, so only its zod-only /schema reach matters). Today’s list / watch (the columnify text) run unchanged — behaviour-preserving in the output, no OpenTUI, no Solid, no new runtime npm deps. The standalone step: it stands up a real bun2nix foundation so PR2a’s bun build bundle lands on proven ground, not a wrapper that would be ripped out. Proof: list / watch (local and --host) emit identical output, now under Bun. kaval-tui stays on tsx; the arivu daemon stays Node. Shipped — #1470 .
  • PR2a — OpenTUI render swap, one endpoint. On the Bun runtime, retire list / watch and their columnify text, and render a simple, SolidJS-canonical OpenTUI list of one endpoint’s terminals. This adds no new connection capability — it dials the already-shipped local --socket (P1c) or a single --host (P2’s dialAgentOnce), reused verbatim; the only new axis (dialing N hosts at once) is PR2b’s. Beside it, a clock that ticks every second is the deliberate liveness proof — createSignal + onMount(() => { const id = setInterval(…); onCleanup(() => clearInterval(id)) }), read in JSX, repainting only the one changed cell — so the whole Bun → OpenTUI (Zig core via Bun.dlopen) → Solid pipeline is proven reactive behind the smallest possible UI. The terminal list itself is a one-shot snapshot at render — it does not yet auto-update on awareness changes; wiring the mirror deltas that make it live is PR2b’s job. arivu-tui --json dumps the flat awareness JSON. This adds @opentui/* + solid-js, the build.ts bundle (Bun.build + @opentui/solid/bun-plugin), and the bunfig.toml dev preload — the only real packaging change, touching only the viewer wrapper. Proof: the OpenTUI list renders a host’s terminals, and the one-second clock is confirmed live by raw-PTY capture (frame N+1 ≠ frame N), not a snapshot-and-grep. (PR2a can lift render.ts’s projection + tones, the data-read helpers, and bin.ts’s output-mode dispatch from the feat/arivu-tui-bun-opentui branch.)
  • PR2b — the multi-host fleet board. On PR2a’s OpenTUI render, fan it across hosts: fan P2’s one-shot per-host dial over N hosts, mirrorRemoteCollection each awareness collection into one aggregate keyed by (host, terminalId), make the list go live (re-render on any mirror delta — the dynamism PR2a deferred), float every awaiting_user agent to the top of the whole fleet, and render the per-host groups + the breathing alert strip + the honest unreachable/skew/empty states. Proof: a live board over 2–3 real boxes, liveness checked by raw-PTY capture; kill one box and watch its header flip to “unreachable” while the others keep updating.

Build constraints — OpenTUI + Solid (land in PR2a, hold through PR2b)

  • OpenTUI repaints only the CHANGED CELL (a cursor move + one character), never the whole string. Verify “is it live?” with raw-PTY capture (or a testRender assertion that frame N+1 ≠ frame N) — never kaval-tui snapshot | grep or a glance, which miss the one ticking character and read a live UI as “frozen”.
  • Pre-bundle; don’t run from source. build.ts (Bun.build + @opentui/solid/bun-plugin) is the official recipe and applies babel-preset-solid exactly as the dev bunfig.toml preload does — the bundle is fully reactive. Running from source with a runtime preload buys nothing and drags @babel / browserslist into the closure. Clean build.ts’s outdir before each build (Bun.build doesn’t, so orphan chunks linger).
  • SolidJS-canonical only. A live value is createSignal + onMount(() => { const id = setInterval(…); onCleanup(() => clearInterval(id)) }), read in JSX. No fps caps, requestRender, externally-driven timers, or process.stderr monkeypatching.
  • Forwarded daemon stderr over --host is quieted at the daemon (warn over --stdio), never with a viewer-side capture hack, so it can’t corrupt the alt-screen.

Why Bun, contained to the viewer

OpenTUI is the production-grade TUI framework — it powers OpenCode and terminal.shop — and it ships a Solid reconciler (@opentui/solid), so the dashboard is authored in the same fine-grained-reactive model kolu already lives in: the mirrored awareness collection becomes Solid signals, <box>/<text> components re-render only the cells that changed, and a useTimeline hook drives animation. The catch — and the whole reason the runtime swap (PR1) comes first — is that OpenTUI’s renderer is a native Zig core loaded via Bun.dlopen: it needs Bun.

So the decision is narrow: arivu-tui alone becomes a Bun binary. The rest of kolu stays Node/tsx, and so does the arivu daemon — it is the half that travels over ssh and leans on Node’s built-in node:sqlite, so it must stay Node, and neither PR touches it. Only the local viewer re-platforms, and that blast radius is exactly what makes the cost acceptable: the viewer is never provisioned over ssh, so the Bun runtime + the per-arch Zig lib live only in the local closure. arivu-tui’s existing deps (@kolu/surface, @kolu/surface-nix-host, the contract) are Node-API code that runs unmodified under Bun’s Node compatibility.

Packaging is not new ground: reuse drishti’s Bun-on-Nix recipe. drishti already ships a Bun server packaged with Nix (and already vendors @kolu/surface out of this repo), so “a Bun binary built cross-arch under Nix” is solved in the kolu orbit — PR1 adopts that recipe wholesale: bun2nix’s fetchBunDeps reads a committed bun.nix (per-tarball FODs, no network in the sandbox), @kolu/* is hydrated post-install exactly as drishti hydrates @kolu/surface, and the bun manifest lives outside the pnpm workspace so the two package managers never fight over one package.json (the viewer keeps its pnpm manifest for typecheck + vitest). PR2a then re-renders the same AwarenessValue fields render.ts shows today through OpenTUI on one host — behaviour-preserving in the data, an upgrade in presentation — and PR2b fans that render across the fleet.

One Bun binary in a Node monorepo — contained on purpose

A deliberate, bounded runtime split, not a drift. It honours volatility-locality: the thing that forces Bun (OpenTUI’s FFI renderer) is a local-viewer concern, so the Bun runtime stays inside the viewer’s wrapper and never leaks into a shared package, the daemon closure, or anything that crosses ssh. The daemon — the piece with the hard portability constraint (node:sqlite, cross-arch travel) — stays Node. The Bun-ness is one wrapper, one binary: easy to read, easy to undo, and the packaging already graduated (it’s drishti’s) the day a second Bun consumer appears.

The fleet board

Now the multi-host generalisation (PR2b), on the OpenTUI render PR2a lands. arivu-tui fleet --host a --host b --host c (hosts also readable from ~/.ssh/config or a small ~/.config/arivu/fleet.toml, local included by default) fans P2’s one-shot dialer over the host list, mirrors every host’s awareness collection into one in-process aggregate, and renders the union as a compact, live board — grouped by host, with every awaiting_user agent floated to the top of the whole fleet so your eye lands on which agent is blocked on me, and where:

arivu-tui fleet⟳ live · 1s
●2 agents need you— zest · pu-build-7
▌zest· 4 terminals
●claudekolu · feat/dial-ssh #1412 ✓awaiting you3s
◜codexdrishti · masterworking0s
◜claudeanyforge · fix/checks #1408 ✗working4s
○claudenotes · mainidle12m
▌pu-build-7· 2 terminals
●claudekolu · fix/heap-oom #1427 ✓awaiting you9s
◜—infra · deployworking1s
▌staging· unreachableECONNREFUSED
▌local· 1 terminal
○claudearivu · feat/fleetidle2m
● 2 need you◜ 3 working○ 2 idle1 host down

A terminal you keep on the second monitor: when nothing needs you it sits calm — cyan “working” spinners and dim “idle” rows, a quiet all clear ✓. The moment an agent hits awaiting_user, its row lifts to the top of the fleet and a warm amber strip breathes at the top of the board, so a glance from the game tells you someone’s waiting before you’ve read a word. The same board, resting:

arivu-tui fleet⟳ live · 1s
▌zest· 4 terminals
◜codexdrishti · masterworking0s
◜claudeanyforge · fix/checks #1408 ✗working4s
○claudenotes · mainidle12m
▌pu-build-7· 2 terminals
◜claudekolu · fix/heap-oom #1427 ✓working9s
◜—infra · deployworking1s
▌staging· unreachableECONNREFUSED
▌local· 1 terminal
○claudearivu · feat/fleetidle2m
● 0 need you◜ 4 working○ 2 idleall clear ✓

Default grouping is by host (a header per box carrying its terminal count and a reachable/skew/unreachable badge); within and across the fleet the rows sort “needs-you” first — every awaiting_user agent bubbles to the top regardless of which box it’s on, so the operator’s eye lands on which agent is blocked on me, and where. A --by agent flip regroups across hosts into one fleet-wide urgency list (“show me every agent waiting on input, anywhere”), which is the actual team-monitor question. --watch keeps it live (re-render on any mirror delta); --json emits the flat [{ host, terminalId, ...AwarenessValue }] for scripting (e.g. a notifier that pings when any box has an awaiting agent). The honest empty/degraded states are first-class — an unreachable host, a version-skew host, and a zero-terminal host each render distinctly rather than silently vanishing.

How it composes the arivu surface — four moves, each reusing a shipped seam; the only genuinely-new code is the cross-host join.

  • Discover hosts (bin layer, no new transport). Resolve from explicit --host flags, an ~/.ssh/config parse (drop wildcard * stanzas), or fleet.toml; merge local unless --no-local. No host-discovery protocol is invented — lean on the box list the way the .gitignore/source-of-truth-reuse instinct demands.
  • Dial + provision per host (reuse P2 verbatim). For each remote host call the same one-shot connectArivuViaHost(host) P2 builds in arivu-tui/src/hostConnect.ts — getHostSession → nix copy the cross-arch arivu daemon closure → ssh <host> arivu --stdio → a transport-blind Connection, with the version-cell read as the connectivity probe (P2’s markConnected seam; arivu has no system.heartbeat). For local, call the existing connectArivu(socketPath). P3 adds only the fan-out: Promise.allSettled over the host list, so a cold-provisioning box (a multi-minute nix copy) and a fast one connect in parallel and one dead box can’t sink the others.
  • Mirror each host’s awareness collection. For every live Connection, run the shipped mirrorRemoteCollection<TerminalId, AwarenessValue> against that client’s awareness collection — the same primitive kolu’s R-2 fold will consume and the remote-process-monitor demo already proved. Its upserts/removes write into one in-process aggregate Map<HostKey, Map<TerminalId, AwarenessValue>>, so agent-state transitions flow live across every box with no polling.
  • Aggregate / key-by-host. Host is stamped onto each mirrored row at the dial site — the fleet knows which connection a row came from, and the daemon never authors a host id, exactly as arivu’s core model requires (AwarenessValue deliberately carries no location/hostId; a daemon can’t know its own kolu-side key). This is the awareness-layer echo of kolu’s kolu-side location stamp. The render layer reads the aggregate keyed by (host, terminalId), groups under per-host headers, applies the cross-fleet needs-you sort, and repaints on any delta.

Why this is arivu-native — it is the awareness analog of drishti, and that parallel is the whole point. Per electricity, @kolu/surface proved it was real electricity not by being extracted but by graduating to a second consumer in drishti — a fleet process-monitor (browser ↔ Bun ↔ ssh-agent) that mirrors per-host process surfaces and keys by host, with none of kolu’s domain. arivu’s P3 is the exact same shape one layer up: a fleet dashboard that mirrors N remote arivu daemons into one host-keyed view, proving arivu’s own surface (the awareness collection + the version cell) is itself a receptacle a different app plugs into. Crucially, nothing in @kolu/arivu-contract changes for P3 to exist — that invariance under a brand-new multi-host consumer is the electricity proof (test ③ of electricity made real for the arivu surface). It is a genuinely different app from the single-host viewer (one host → N, vertical record → aggregated board), and the contract never notices it went from 1 host to N. And it is the honest home for the user’s “remote kolu monitor / agent-status dashboard”: a team-wide, read-only monitor is a different product from kolu’s own-canvas fold (remote-terminals R-2’s job) — so it belongs as arivu’s own consumer, with zero kolu-server, not folded into kolu.

A second-order consumer — and why it stays a TUI, not a surface-app

The drishti parallel proves the surface framework is electricity; the fleet board proves the arivu surface, built on that framework, is itself electricity — a second-order receptacle. The temptation will be to make this a drishti-shaped web board, dragging in the whole @kolu/surface-app shell. P3 resists that: a read-only aggregator has nothing to fold and nothing to persist, so a browser shell with a stale-tab/reconnect story is pure ceremony for a glance view. The TUI fleet mode ships fast, is dev-loop-native, and proves the aggregation core cheaply. A web arivu-board (the literal drishti twin — browser ↔ Bun ↔ N ssh dials, the third consumer of @kolu/surface-app) is a real, attractive follow-on, but it is explicitly second and gated behind the TUI proving the core — purely additive, never the headline.

Rough shape — no new @kolu/* package. OpenTUI (@opentui/core + @opentui/solid) is the renderer electricity, taken as an external dependency per prefer-libraries rather than hand-rolled; PR2a swaps it in (on PR1’s Bun runtime) for the one-endpoint list, and PR2b adds the fleet subcommand and the cross-host aggregate on the existing packages/arivu-tui bin (already on @kolu/surface-nix-host + the contract + the mirror after P2). The artifacts below are PR2b’s, except where noted.

  • bin.ts (edit — PR2a then PR2b) — PR2a drops list/watch and routes the default invocation (local --socket or one --host) to the OpenTUI list (plus --json); PR2b adds a fleet subcommand taking repeated --host <ssh> (and/or --ssh-config / --config), --no-local, --by <needs|host|agent>, --watch, and --json.
  • render.tsx (new — PR2a) — the one-endpoint OpenTUI/Solid view: a <box>/<text> list of the endpoint’s terminals plus the one-second clock signal that proves the render loop is live. The pure projection (the agentValue/prValueText/relativeTime/agentStatusLabel row helpers lifted from today’s render.ts) stays plain data, unit-tested under Node/vitest; OpenTUI only paints it. PR2b reuses these helpers for the fleet groups.
  • fleet.ts (new — PR2b) — the orchestrator: resolve hosts → Promise.allSettled of connectArivuViaHost / connectArivu → per-host mirrorRemoteCollection into one aggregate Map<HostKey, Map<TerminalId, AwarenessValue>> → live repaint; owns the host-keying stamp and the dispose-all-on-exit.
  • hosts.ts (new) — pure host discovery (--host parse, ~/.ssh/config Host stanzas with wildcards dropped, local merge); unit-tested with no I/O.
  • fleetRender (new) — the OpenTUI/Solid view: per-host groups, the cross-fleet needs-you sort, the breathing alert strip + working spinners, the summary line, and the distinct unreachable/skew/empty host states. The pure projection (sort, grouping, the agentValue/prValueText/relativeTime/agentStatusLabel row helpers from render.ts) stays plain data, unit-tested under Node/vitest; OpenTUI only paints it — so the testable logic never depends on the Bun renderer.
  • fleet.test.ts + fleetRender.test.ts (new) — mock connectArivuViaHost (as P2’s hostConnect.test.ts mocks getHostSession) so two fake in-process arivu clients feed the aggregate; assert the grouping, the needs-you sort, and the partial-failure render with no socket.

Nix: PR1 re-points the arivu-tui wrapper from tsx/node --import to Bun; PR2a adds OpenTUI’s per-arch native lib via the build.ts bundle (the only real packaging change, touching only the viewer wrapper); PR2b adds no new packaging — the fleet provisions the same Node arivu --stdio daemon P2 already bakes (ARIVU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON), it just dials more of them. The daemon’s Nix closure is untouched and stays Node.

Two boundary calls keep this honest. The renderer is electricity — a domain-agnostic Solid→terminal capability — but it already exists as OpenTUI, so P3 consumes that receptacle rather than minting a @kolu/solid-tui of its own (a Solid-on-Node renderer was the road not taken once Bun was on the table). The aggregation join, by contrast, is a leaf reducer, not electricity — it hides a bounded host-keyed merge, not a hard volatility (transport + provision + reconnect is already owned by @kolu/surface-nix-host and mirrorRemoteCollection). So PR2b must not extract a @kolu/fleet-mirror: a generic host-keyed merge is exactly the “tidy generic helper” trap electricity warns against.

Fleet proof — dial 2–3 real boxes and see one aggregated fleet.

  • Real proof (the bar every arivu phase holds): on 2–3 pu/prod boxes each running kaval + arivu with a live agent or two, arivu-tui fleet --host <a> --host <b> --host <c> renders every host’s terminals in one screen, with any awaiting_user agent sorted to the top across hosts, and live working→awaiting transitions repainting without a re-dial — capture a screenshot/transcript of the multi-host board, plus the --by agent fleet-wide “who is waiting on input” view.
  • Negative proof matters too (the no-fallback rule — a caught dial error must surface, not collapse to empty): kill one box and confirm its header flips to “unreachable” while the other host’s rows keep updating (partial observability is honest, never a vanished section); point at a version-skew box to confirm the degraded “skew” header.
  • Unit proof (cheap, CI): the mocked two-fake-client test asserts the aggregate is correctly (host, terminalId)-keyed (two boxes’ identical terminal ids stay distinct), the needs-you sort, and that one rejecting dial degrades to a per-host error row rather than sinking the board — the same mock harness P2’s hostConnect.test.ts established.

Retires: that arivu’s served surface is genuinely a second-order receptacle — a different app (the fleet aggregator) plugs into the same contract the single-host viewer uses, with zero kolu-server and no contract change, the awareness-layer analog of how drishti retired the same risk for @kolu/surface.

Status

Proposed — a clean-slate replacement for the awareness layer, child of kaval-sessions. It supersedes the earlier from-scratch plan ( #1398 , closed) and the in-process provider extraction ( #1409 , closed): both kept awareness inside kolu and split hairs over an in-process compose-vs-serve boundary that this design dissolves by moving the producer into a daemon. Built from an adversarial, cost-blind design panel (verdict: sound, with the asymmetry-is-encapsulated and mirror-is-a-fold corrections folded in above), grounded by reading the shipped code: the in-server sensor set (now @kolu/terminal-awareness/sensors.ts), mirrorRemoteCollection, the remote-process-monitor “define once, implement twice” demo, and kaval’s provisioning recipe. The substrate it stands on — kaval (untouched), location as a first-class discriminated union — is already merged. The long pole is P1a → P1b → P1c → P2, and P1a (the behavior-preserving extraction) is the recommended first move, then P1b (finish the leaf rename), then P1c (the standalone arivu + arivu-tui): independently useful, and it retires the biggest risk before kolu depends on a line of it. (P3 — the arivu-tui fleet dashboard — fans P2 out to N hosts; the kolu mirror + fold lives in remote-terminals R-2.) There is no local-compose phase — locally kolu runs the sensor library in-process (settled at P1a); the surface and the fold are remote-only (P2).

P1a shipped as #1413 — @kolu/terminal-awareness is extracted, the schema home inverted, kolu-server re-pointed at it, and the package’s own surface renamed to the sensor · signal · sink vocabulary (startProviders → startAwareness), behaviour-identical. (The kavalChannels.ts kaval-dial bridge listed above moved to P1c: it is the daemon’s connective tissue — kolu-server builds its sensor signals from its own pty-host taps in local.ts, so P1a never needed it.) P1b shipped as #1419 — it finished the provider → adapter rename through the anyagent/anyforge leaves (a single adapter spine, no probe; the Watcher handle and the wire provider discriminant stayed), behaviour-preserving. P1c shipped ( #1428 , merged 2026-06-19) — the standalone arivu (the daemon) + arivu-tui (the viewer), built on the lifted sensor set: arivu dials kaval as a plain ptyHostSurface client, runs the unchanged sensors per terminal, and serves each slice into the @kolu/arivu-contract awareness collection; ephemeral (no gate, no PTY ownership), zero kolu-server at runtime. --stdio is the seam P2’s ssh dial speaks to. P2 shipped ( #1439 ) — arivu-tui --host, the standalone remote viewer; the gauntlet lifted the shared one-shot dial into @kolu/surface-nix-host’s dialAgentOnce, so kaval-tui and arivu-tui are thin wrappers over one primitive. P3 · PR1 shipped ( #1470 ) — the Bun re-platform of the viewer, via bun2nix adopted now rather than deferred: bun2nix pinned via npins and resolved with builtins.getFlake (no flake input — the odu path, so the zero-flake-input rule holds), an isolated bun.lock + bun.nix dep cache kept outside the pnpm workspace, and the @kolu/* closure hydrated from the store (the heavy sensor manifest never bun-installed — only terminal-awareness’s zod-only /schema reach matters). list / watch (local and --host) emit identical output, now under Bun; kaval-tui stays tsx, the daemon stays Node. PR2 is split in two. P3 · PR2a is in review ( #1479 ) — the OpenTUI render swap against one endpoint at a time (the already-shipped local --socket or single --host, reused verbatim — no new connection capability; list/watch and the text path dropped; the awareness table read once from the daemon, with a one-second header clock as the liveness proof; --json dumps the awareness array; the rows stay a one-shot snapshot), proving the Bun → OpenTUI(native Zig via Bun.dlopen) → Solid pipeline on PR1’s bun2nix base. Next: P3 · PR2b wires the multi-host fleet board (fan-out + mirror + the cross-fleet needs-you sort + live deltas) on top. Viewer-only Bun throughout; the daemon stays Node. See the P3 section above for the PRs and the OpenTUI/Solid build constraints. The kolu mirror + fold is not arivu’s job — it is how remote-terminals R-2 consumes arivu (long-lived HostSession, mirrorRemoteCollection, the persisted/live fold), and it lives in that note, gated on R-2.

Referenced by

  • Remote terminals over SSH
← the Atlas github ↗
